25 bare unto him, Manasseh, and Ephraim—three. And the sons of Benjamin: Bela and Becher,

matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Radak on I Chronicles 8:1:1
matches.x: Benjamin begot Bela his first-born, Ashbel the second. The text says “Ashbel the second” when he was really the third; for the sons of Benjamin were Bela, Becher, and Ashbel (Gen 46:21). Indeed, above it does say “Bela, Becher” (7:6). But because it does not mention Becher [here], it says “Ashbel the second.” It does not mention Becher here much as it does not mention him in parashat Pineḥas—just: “Of Bela, the clan of the Belaites; of Ashbel, the clan of the Ashbelites” (Num 26:38). Now in para

“And the sons of Benjamin: Bela, and Bekher, and Ashbel, Gera, and Naaman, Eḥi, and Rosh, Mupim, and Ḥupim, and Ard” (Genesis 46:21). “And the sons of Benjamin: Bela…” – when Joseph stood with Benjamin, he asked him: ‘Do you have sons?’ He said to him: ‘Yes.’ He said to him: ‘How many?’ He said to him: ‘Ten.’ He said: ‘What are their names?’ He said to him: ‘I had one brother. His actions were fine and pleasant, and he was taken captive from me, and I gave them names based on his experience. “Be
The sons of Binyomin [were]: Bela, Becher, Ashbeil, Geiroh, Naamon, Eichi, Rosh, Muppim, Chuppim and Ard.
The sons of Benjamin: Bela, Bekher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Mupim, Hupim, and Ard.

כי נכמרו רחמיו FOR HIS COMPASSION WAS EXCITED — He asked him, “Have you a brother of the same mother as yourself?” — He replied, “I had a brother but I know not where he is”. — “Have you sons?” He replied, “I have ten”. Joseph asked him, “And what are their names?” Benjamin replied, “Bela, Becher etc.” (cf. 46:21). Joseph then enquired, “What are the ideas underlying these names?” He replied, “They all have some reference to my brother and the troubles that have befallen him. I called them Bela

But again we may ask, how is this to be squared with the tradition that Joseph, when he came down to Egypt, asked him whether he had a wife and children, and he answered, “Yes, and they are all named in memory of my brother, to wit, Bela and Becher, and Ashbel, Gera, and Naaman, etc.” (Gen. 46, 21). How, then, can it be said that he abstained from conjugal relations?

The Scriptures did not change their name, and their name was called based on the merit of their family. matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Radak on I Chronicles 7:21:1
matches.x: The men of Gath killed them, the ones born in the land. Some explain (Pseudo-Rashi) that the reason the text says “the ones born in the land” is that the men of Gath were born in the land, and knew the lay of the land; so when the Ephraimites came into the land to take their cattle and did not know the roads, the men of Gath ambushed them and killed them. And some (?) explain that it says “the ones born in the land” because Gath later belonged to Israel; so it says that the men of Gath who were

21 and Arodi—eight. And the sons of Asher: Imnah, and Ishvah, (and Ishvi), and Beriah, and Serah,
The sons of Asher [were]: Yimnah, Yishvah, Yishvi, and Beriah, and their sister, Serach. The sons of Beriah [were]: Chever and Malki’eil.

The sons of Asher: Yimna, Yishva, Yishvi, Beria, and Serah their sister. Perhaps this granddaughter is mentioned because she was the only one born in Canaan. Alternatively, there were other unidentified granddaughters as well, but Serah had a unique personality. The Sages relate that she was very close to Jacob and that she merited numerous blessings, including exceptional longevity. And the sons of Beria: Hever and Malkiel.

The children of Asher, Yimna, Yishvah, Yishvi, Beriah and Serach their sister, who was carried away while alive into the Garden (of Eden), because she had announced to Jakob that Joseph still lived. It was she who saved the inhabitants of (the city) Abel from the judgment of death, in the days of Joab. The sons of Beriah who went down into Mizraim were Heber and Malkiel.
